response.description_long:
Forkfleet runs a two-sided marketplace for used commercial kitchen equipment — ovens, ranges, refrigeration, prep tables, espresso machines — in a market that historically cleared through auctioneers and local dealers at steep discounts. The structural problem it names is symmetric: closing or renovating restaurants dump equipment for little, while buyers face a used market with no condition standards and no delivery.

The product standardizes both trust and logistics. Sellers photograph equipment in the app and get a price estimate built from comparable completed sales in their market; a Forkfleet inspector then verifies condition against a written, public protocol — burners fired, compressor amperage measured, door seals and temperature calibration checked — before the listing goes live with a graded condition report. Grade A means no functional issues, Grade B cosmetic wear, Grade C a disclosed and priced-in defect. Buyers see the report, financing terms from a lending partner, and a firm delivery quote; freight runs through partner carriers with liftgate delivery, inside placement, and haul-away of old equipment.

The economics are stated plainly: sellers pay an 8 percent commission, buyers pay a flat service fee, and nothing is consigned to a warehouse — equipment ships directly from seller to buyer, which keeps the company asset-light while the inspection layer lets it stand behind a 30-day working guarantee on every purchase. A 48-hour acceptance window covers the delivery itself, and the site publishes guarantee claims rates by category, with Grade B refrigeration running under 3 percent.

Traction markers are specific: launched in Chicago in 2019, now in 12 metro markets — from Milwaukee and Minneapolis through Nashville, Atlanta, and Dallas — with over 5,000 items listed at any time. Buyers purchase from anywhere in the continental US; sellers list where inspector coverage exists, and new markets open only when it does. About a fifth of listings come from licensed dealers, who operate under the same inspection standard.

Forkfleet was founded by Nate Kowalski, a former equipment dealer and auctioneer, and Maria Reyes, who built logistics software for a freight brokerage — a pairing that matches the founding insight that the market needed condition standards and freight, not another listing site. The company is 45 people, headquartered in Chicago, IL, with inspectors and market managers in each metro. The whole-kitchen flow for closures — one inspector visit cataloging an entire kitchen — remains its busiest use case.
